title: I got tired of spending weeks on backtests so I built something that runs thousands of combinations in minutes. Here's what I learned from the first 10,000 strategies it tested.
author: Important_Buy626
selftext:
A bit of background: I trade forex and crypto. For years my process was get an idea, manually backtest it, tweak parameters one by one, repeat for weeks.

It was slow, it was biased, and I kept gravitating toward the parameter set that looked best rather than the one that was most robust.

So I built a system that takes a strategy idea and tests it across thousands of parameter combinations automatically then surfaces the versions that perform consistently, not just the ones that look great on a specific date range.

After running this on thousands of real strategies here's what surprised me most:

The top-performing backtest result was almost never in the top 20% for live performance Strategies that worked across wide parameter ranges outperformed tight-parameter "perfect" strategies by a significant margin in live trading The biggest predictor of live failure wasn't wrong market assumptions it was overfitting to a specific time window

Happy to go deep on any of this. What are you currently using to stress-test strategies before going live?

comments.author: Dependent_Stay_6954
comments.body:
This is one of the better trading posts I’ve seen on Reddit because you’re actually discussing robustness and overfitting instead of just posting “AI alpha” screenshots.

The point about broad parameter stability outperforming highly optimised parameter sets is strongly supported in quant literature. In my own testing pipelines, the “perfect” backtests were very often the least stable live candidates.

I’ve been building an institutional-style validation framework myself (V8A pipeline) and one of the biggest findings was exactly this: once you start running thousands of parameter combinations, overfitting becomes the central problem, not signal generation.

In one recent run:

- ~4.3 million raw signals processed
- ~6,000 backtest rows generated
- ~5,200 walk-forward rows
- Benjamini–Hochberg FDR correction applied across the candidate set
- only 13 survivors after multiple-testing adjustment
- and ultimately only 1 edge passed the full institutional validation stack

That alone shows how dangerous raw optimisation can be. Most apparent “edges” disappear once you apply:

- walk-forward validation,
- holdout testing,
- Monte Carlo,
- autocorrelation-adjusted Sharpe,
- and multiple-testing correction.

One thing I’d be interested in from your side is whether your robustness regions survive after:

- out-of-sample walk-forward,
- regime segmentation,
- and FDR / Reality Check style adjustments.

Because I completely agree that parameter stability matters — but entire parameter regions can still become overfit if the search space is large enough.

Conceptually though, this is far closer to proper quant thinking than most retail trading posts.
